coredata.dc:title: Introducing a new model for solid-state batteries: Parameter estimation and sensitivity analysis on diffusion, concentration, and electrochemical kinetics
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Iwakiri, Igor Gabriel Ito
Delgado, Nuno M.
Nogueira, Idelfonso B.R.
coredata.dc:description:
Despite their potential, solid-state batteries (SSBs) are challenging to optimize due to the complexity of their materials and the early stage of their modeling, especially compared to liquid-electrolyte technologies. Many existing models rely on empirical equations that cannot represent internal mass-transport and kinetic phenomena. Even studies based on mechanistic models often focus on theoretical explanations of experimental phenomena or generating hard-to-obtain experimental data. In this work, a simple yet versatile mechanistic model – able to simulate any battery composed of a metallic anode, solid electrolyte and intercalation cathode – is proposed and used in a parameter estimation routine to identify the material properties of a Li/LiPON/
LiCoO
2
battery. After validation, a parametric study is made to address how each material property impacts concentration profiles, discharge curves, overpotentials, and core key-performance indexes (KPIs) related to energy efficiency and battery capacity. Findings reveal that cathode diffusion is critical to enhancing Extraction Efficiency, in some cases reaching 98.9% of the theoretical capacity without enhancing any other parameter. In the case of Energy Efficiency being a priority over capacity, electrolyte diffusion and concentration showed a pivotal role, in the range of parameters studied, to increase Voltaic Efficiency up to 97.8%. Despite not directly affecting the Extraction Efficiency, it is discussed how high overpotentials caused by Electrolyte Concentration and Kinetic Constants still can harm battery capacity, for charge–discharge cycles limited by boundaries in electrical potential. Finally, studies simultaneously varying two or more parameters show that, despite extreme property enhancements (up to ca 500x higher than the standard case) indeed lead to exceptional results (up to 96.4% for Voltaic Efficiency and 98.9% for Extraction Efficiency), to use such materials at its full potential will result in other zones of the device usually neglected – such as the overpotentials from the metal anode or even the current collector – may become the new stumbling block, underscoring the significance of considering the battery as a holistic system that will inherently exhibit bottlenecks. This also implies, however, in the perpetual potential for enhancement across its components.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-state battery
Material properties
Electrochemical modeling
Energy efficiency

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Despite their potential, solid-state batteries (SSBs) are challenging to optimize due to the complexity of their materials and the early stage of their modeling, especially compared to liquid-electrolyte technologies. Many existing models rely on empirical equations that cannot represent internal mass-transport and kinetic phenomena. Even studies based on mechanistic models often focus on theoretical explanations of experimental phenomena or generating hard-to-obtain experimental data. In this work, a simple yet versatile mechanistic model – able to simulate any battery composed of a metallic anode, solid electrolyte and intercalation cathode – is proposed and used in a parameter estimation routine to identify the material properties of a Li/LiPON/
LiCoO
2
battery. After validation, a parametric study is made to address how each material property impacts concentration profiles, discharge curves, overpotentials, and core key-performance indexes (KPIs) related to energy efficiency and battery capacity. Findings reveal that cathode diffusion is critical to enhancing Extraction Efficiency, in some cases reaching 98.9% of the theoretical capacity without enhancing any other parameter. In the case of Energy Efficiency being a priority over capacity, electrolyte diffusion and concentration showed a pivotal role, in the range of parameters studied, to increase Voltaic Efficiency up to 97.8%. Despite not directly affecting the Extraction Efficiency, it is discussed how high overpotentials caused by Electrolyte Concentration and Kinetic Constants still can harm battery capacity, for charge–discharge cycles limited by boundaries in electrical potential. Finally, studies simultaneously varying two or more parameters show that, despite extreme property enhancements (up to ca 500x higher than the standard case) indeed lead to exceptional results (up to 96.4% for Voltaic Efficiency and 98.9% for Extraction Efficiency), to use such materials at its full potential will result in other zones of the device usually neglected – such as the overpotentials from the metal anode or even the current collector – may become the new stumbling block, underscoring the significance of considering the battery as a holistic system that will inherently exhibit bottlenecks. This also implies, however, in the perpetual potential for enhancement across its components.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of solid-state batteries marks a significant milestone in energy storage technologies, presenting a promising alternative to traditional liquid electrolyte batteries by offering advantages of enhanced safety, improved efficiency, thermal stability, and higher energy density [1], which are crucial for meeting the growing energy storage demands across various sectors, including portable electronics and electric vehicles [2]. Despite their potential, unlocking the full capabilities of solid-state batteries is a complex challenge, primarily due to the complex nature of their materials and internal mechanisms [3,4]. Modeling is a critical component in the advancement and fine-tuning of solid-state batteries. It serves as an essential tool for predicting behavior, deciphering the mechanisms at play, and steering experimental designs [5]. However, the field of solid-state battery modeling is moderately undeveloped, especially compared to the more mature domain of liquid electrolyte battery modeling. This disparity stems, among other reasons, from the diverse and complex phenomena characteristic of solid-state systems [6–8], which make it difficult for existing models and equations to capture their dynamics accurately and, as a result, most of the models proposed in the literature are still divergent.

For example, despite some works claiming that it is enough to use the simple diffusion law to describe the ion movement in the cathode zone (e.g., [9]), others could claim that it is necessary to consider two species moving – ion and counter ion – and solve the system through the electroneutrality hypothesis (e.g., [10] which ultimately also impact the boundary conditions of the system since in the latter case there is transport of species i.e. counter ion, electrons between the cathode and current-collector. Nonetheless, there is also disagreement regarding the necessity of correcting the diffusion coefficient of such species, while some works claim that it can be considered approximately constant (e.g., [11]) whereas other sources may adapt it according the state-of-charge (e.g., [12]), or even the amount of damage (e.g., [13]). Also, there is no convergence regarding how to model the electrolyte zone, where some studies use simple diffusion law (e.g., [14]) while others argue the necessity of considering the dissociation reaction of such electrolyte, as in ([15; 16]).

Also, many of the models dedicated to solid-state batteries rely on empirical equations designed to simulate system-level behavior [17]. This approach simplifies the modeling process but at the cost of versatility: with no phenomenological equation/relationship, the simplification leads to a disconnect between macro-level outcomes, such as charge–discharge curves, and the fundamental parameters of the materials involved. Consequently, these models generally compromise the versatility to apply their findings to new materials with known experimental properties [18]. This is because empirical or even circuit-oriented battery models often rely on mathematical approximations rather than a detailed, phenomenological understanding of mass transport and electrochemical reactions within the battery [19].

Moreover, while some research efforts have developed phenomenological electrochemical models to bridge this gap, they tend to focus on generating results that would be challenging to acquire experimentally for specific materials, such as concentration profiles that would be problematic to be obtained by, for example, in situ Neutron Depth Profile (NDP), or even use such models to explain experimentally observed phenomena from a theoretical point of view [10]. This approach, however, often neglects to thoroughly investigate how variations in material properties influence the generated data, limiting our understanding of the material’s impact not only on the device behavior in terms of concentration and charge–discharge profiles but also on common metrics related to their energy efficiency and capacity. Also, even the studies that proposed parametric analyses to cover such gap emphasize geometric parameters, such as thickness, and even when addressing other material properties, they do not explain, from an electrochemical point of view, the results presented [9]. For clarity, Table 1 presents a brief comparison between papers proposing Solid-State Battery Models.

To address these shortcomings, our study presents a phenomenological model (i.e. explicitly based on material properties), generic to any solid-state battery with metal anode and intercalation cathode. The model is validated for a Li
x
CoO
2
/LiPON/Li device, and finally, an in-depth parametric analysis is conducted, aiming to thoroughly examine how material properties such as diffusion coefficients, kinetics, and concentrations influence the ion concentration profiles and sources of energy loss, identifying and utilizing Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) relevant to real-word applications and clarifying how each material property affects these KPIs. Our study provides insights into the complex relationships between materials’ fundamental properties and device behavior, thereby contributing to future works in optimizing and enhancing their performance.

In this section, we present the proposed model along with all the hypotheses and assumptions used, which are explicitly mentioned, addressing where, how, and why they were considered. Nonetheless, for the sake of convenience, such considerations are also briefly summarized as:

ce:list-item.ce:para: The metallic anode is always composed of the metallic form of the ion on which the battery is based.
Shunt-currents and side reactions are non-existent, therefore the external current contributes to the redox reactions in its entirety.

In the Electrolyte Zone, both the species representing the ion and the vacancy resulting from its dissociation are under the electroneutrality hypothesis, therefore presenting the same concentration but different physical properties, such as diffusion.
The electrolyte material presents a self-dissociation reaction, and the resulting ion and vacancy will always present the same stoichiometric coefficient and opposite charges.
In the Cathode Zone, the intercalated ion and the electron are also under the electroneutrality hypothesis.
The Cathode Zone will be considered as a pseudo-homogeneous material, even though in practice some electrolyte particles are inside such region.

The cathode material suffers structural changes for different state-of-charges, resulting in a variation of the diffusion coefficient of all species in its domain, therefore a correction factor is applied as
β = f
( y , t )
.
The mass transport in all sections can be represented by the Nernst–Planck equation, therefore described by its diffusion and migration components.
The ohmic resistance of the metallic anode is considered constant over time, as the variation in its overpotential is negligible compared to other sources.
No degradation mechanism is considered in this work.

ce:section.ce:para: The system to be considered represents an intercalation (layered) cathode, a solid-state electrolyte, and a metallic anode. A graphical representation of the system is depicted in Fig. 1.

Since the model presented in this work can represent any system following such component structure, a generalistic nomenclature is used during the theoretical sections. Thus, the electrochemical reactions expected to occur are delineated by Eq. (1) and Eq. (2):

For illustrative purposes, several cathode materials that can be simulated using the proposed model are enumerated in Table 2, alongside the corresponding nomenclature adopted in this study.

Notice that, considering the anticipated expansion/contraction of the anode material due to its reduction/oxidation throughout the discharging and charging cycles, the origin of the x -axis is positioned at the interface between the anode and the solid electrolyte for simplicity — as observed in Fig. 1.

Considering the development presented in this section, a few takeaways are worth to be highlighted:

ce:list-item.ce:para:
Advantages. The major advantage of using the electroneutrality hypothesis is that, as long as both species present opposite charges (i.e.
z
M
+
=
z
N
−
), the migration term can be nullified, therefore making the Mass Balance in the Electrolyte independent of the Electrical Field. Otherwise, such a system would turn out to be problematic to be numerically solved, specifically the
∂ a n E
∂ y
component.

Mobility. Even though the electroneutrality hypothesis implies that both species present the same concentration, their diffusion coefficients, elemental charge, and boundary conditions are still different. This means that, despite both concentration profiles progressing the same way, if we “trace” one particle of
M
+
, it would be possible to see it crossing through and beyond the Electrolyte Zone. The vacancies, however, are static in that despite their local concentration changing over time, it is a mere consequence of the electrolyte dissociation reaction and
M
+
movement, therefore the vacancies could never leave the Electrolyte Zone.

“Equivalent Species”. Notice that Eq. (22) resembles a Mass Balance of an arbitrary “Equivalent” species in which the diffusion and reaction are considered. The migration is neglected, where an “Equivalent Diffusion Coefficient” would be a combination of both species under the electroneutrality hypothesis and equal to

Boundary Conditions. Finally, it is worth calling attention to the fact that, even though the concentrations of both species are linked by the electroneutrality hypothesis, the Boundary Conditions are independent of

In this subsection, we endeavor to delineate three concepts that, while not unfamiliar to the informed reader, warrant precise definitions to ensure the clarity of the discussions that follow. By doing so, we aim to lay a clear and common groundwork for the detailed analysis and nuanced argumentation that are integral to the forthcoming sections of this paper.

The C-rate (
h
− 1
) is a normalized measure of the current at which a battery is charged or discharged, relative to its maximum capacity. In this work, we adopted the nomenclature of a negative C-Rate for the Discharging process and a positive C-Rate for the Charging process. This concept is mathematically defined as:

η

and thus the discharge curve would coincide with the Equilibrium Curve. It is also possible to estimate the ideal battery capacity from the definition of Faraday Constant:

(1)), n is the stoichiometric coefficient that relates the mols of electrons reacted per mol of ions (Dimensionless), F is the Faraday Constant (C mol−1), and the 3.6 on the denominator is a conversion factor to translate the Faraday Constant to (mAh mol−1).

The Extraction Efficiency is a normalized measure of the battery real capacity normalized by the battery ideal capacity defined in Eq. (36). The battery real capacity can be defined by the amount of charge that the battery can receive (during the charge step) or deliver (during the discharge step). It can be defined by:

The Voltaic Efficiency is a normalized measure of the energy per ion reacted that the battery can store (during the charging process) or deliver (during the discharge process) normalized by the energy per ion in case of no electrical potential losses i.e.
η
total
= 0
and thus the charge/discharge curve would coincide with the Equilibrium Curve predicted from thermodynamics. Such an index differs from Energy Efficiency since it does not take into account Coulombic losses (such as shunt-currents). In case there are no Coulombic losses and all the electrons from the external current imply into an ion charged/discharged in the battery main reactions (as in the case of this work) the Coulombic Efficiency is equal to one and the Voltaic Efficiency coincides with the Electrical Efficiency. Voltaic Efficiency can be defined as:

To estimate the parameters of each material for a given set of experimental results, an optimization problem is proposed by reducing the root mean squared deviation (RMSD) between the experimental results and the simulation results for a given set of parameters, as represented by the loss function ( Φ ) in:
(41)

Lithium phosphorus oxynitride (LiPON) is commonly used as a solid-electrolyte for being less sensitive to air and the stability of its solid-electrolyte interface (SEI) with metal lithium. On another hand, the lithium cobalt oxide cathode is widely used for battery applications mostly for its high specific capacity, low self-discharge, and excellent life cycle. Considering the scarcity of experimental results reported for Solid-State Batteries, the dataset presented from Raijmakers et al. [10] was used due to the availability of charge–discharge data at several different C-Rates. The authors cycled a 0.7 mAh Li/LiPON/
LiCoO
2
thin-film all-solid-state Li-ion battery for such a dataset. In the tests, the authors performed a CC-charge at 1 C-rate until the battery reached 4.2 V followed by CV-charging using a cut-off current of 0.1 C-Rate (i.e. approximately 100% state-of-charge, x = 0.5 as in
Li
x
CoO
2
). Then, the discharge curves would be performed at different C-Rates = [-6, -4, -2, −0.2] C.

To represent such a system using the model proposed in this work, the parameters necessary to describe the Equilibrium Curve and the ones corresponding to
β
( y , t )
were extracted from the same paper in which the referred dataset was obtained. Notice that, despite the parametric analysis implying the simulation of a “theoretical material”, with arbitrary properties, the parameter
β
( y , t )
is based specifically on the lithium cobalt oxide material (i.e. we are assuming that, even though the diffusion coefficient values will vary, the proportion of such change is similar to what is observed for LCO). In contrast, the Nernst Curve is based on lithium cobalt oxide vs. lithium metal as a benchmark. Such a decision was taken primarily due to pragmatic considerations — both functions are inputs where the analysis extends beyond mere parameter variation. Further examination of this aspect is deferred to subsequent studies.

For the Parameter Estimation, the dataset referred to in the Case Study section was used, considering 98 experimental points representing the discharge curves for C-Rate = [-6, -4, -2, −0.2] C, as represented by the dots in Fig. 2. Then, the Nelder–Mead routine (cf. Optimization and Problem Formulation) was applied to decrease the RMSD between the experimental values and the four discharge curves simultaneously. The estimated parameters resultants from such a method are presented in Table 3.

Although some of the estimated parameters are not directly available from experimental data in the literature, it is still possible to confirm that most of them align with the values proposed by the original study from which the experimental dataset was sourced [10]. Additionally, these parameters are consistent with findings from other independent studies that utilize experimental techniques such as Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) and Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration Technique (GITT) [23], as well as molecular dynamics simulations [21].

Considering such parameters, four discharge curves (using C-Rate from −0.2 C to −6 C) were simulated and compared with the experimental results. The RMSD utilizing the estimated parameters has an average of ca. 0.06 V per curve, a value ca. 46% lower than the RMSD based on the parameters extracted from the literature, which presented an average of ca. 0.11 V per curve. The results are displayed in Fig. 2.

Thus, it is possible to observe that the proposed parameter estimation methodology was able to converge into values in agreement with results reported in the literature (in both theoretical and experimental papers, as mentioned previously), even though such values were not fed into the optimization algorithm. Furthermore, the model proposed in this work (using such estimated parameters) can satisfactorily describe the discharge curves of the experimental dataset mentioned in the “Case Study” section, with an average RMSD of 0.06 V — considerably lower than the 0.11 V per curve obtained using the so-called “Literature Values” in Table 3.

By observing Fig. 2, it is possible to observe that the deviation between the experimental discharge curve and the values predicted by our model increases for lower electrical potentials — even though such deviation is kept lower than ca. 5% across all the curves. This pattern is observed because the Electrical Potential is based on the Overpotential in a given state (cf. Eq. (33)), and for higher currents other voltage loss phenomena that are not taken into consideration in this work will arise [24] - notice that Experimental Values are lower than Simulated Values.

The first analysis was done by simultaneously varying the diffusion coefficient of the main species (
D
M
⊕
) and the diffusion coefficient of the electron (
D
e
−
) which, in this model, is acting as a counter-ion due to the electroneutrality hypothesis. Such parameters were multiplied by a factor ranging from 0.1 to 500, and the results are displayed in Fig. 3. All the other parameters of Table 3 were kept constant and the C-Rate for this study is always equal to −1 unless stated otherwise.

It is worthwhile to note that the electroneutrality hypothesis (
a
e
−
=
a
M
⊕
) implies the calculation of an “equivalent diffusion coefficient” (
D
p
, cf. Eq. (30)), which drives the mass transport of both species. Also, by multiplying the diffusion coefficient of both species by a constant, the new “equivalent diffusion coefficient” (
D
p
) will present the same value as if it was directly multiplied by such constant. Thus, for the sake of convenience, the term “cathode diffusion” will refer to the equivalent diffusion coefficient unless stated otherwise.

It is possible to observe from Fig. 3 how the cathode diffusion can drastically affect the battery overpotential. As a reference, Case 0.2 presented a voltaic efficiency of ca. 92.7%, compared with the Nernst Curve — which, despite being the lowest value for this set of tests, is still a substantial value. This happened because, for overpotentials infinitely high, an infinite driving force would be necessary to keep the battery discharging, and therefore, the discharge routine is ceased. Since the energy efficiency only considers the interval of time the battery is being effectively discharged (i.e. before the tests are interrupted due to extreme overpotentials), the impact of a poor cathode diffusion on such metric is also limited. The Extraction Efficiency, on the other hand, was ca. 41.1% suggesting that, for the conditions simulated, the biggest influence of a poor diffusion on the cathode zone would not necessarily be the loss of energy during cycling but the premature interruption of the discharge due to the peak in the cathode mass transfer overpotential — ultimately jeopardizing the battery capacity.

It is also worth mentioning that in scenarios in which the cathode diffusion is too high, such as Case 500.0, it was possible to achieve an Extraction Efficiency of ca. 98.9%, indicating that if battery capacity was the only concern, it would be possible to reach values significantly close to ideality just by improving cathode diffusion without altering the electrolyte properties. Still, even for Case 500.0, the battery total overpotential will get gradually closer to a limit that cannot be surpassed just by modifications in the cathode material — only achieving an Energy Efficiency of ca. 95.3%. The impact of such modifications in the concentration profile, as well as the overpotential sources causing the deviation between the discharge curves and the Nernst Curve are further analyzed in Fig. 4.

By observing the flattening phenomena across Figs. 4 (g, d, a), it is possible to conclude that higher values of cathode diffusion (
D
p
) will not only lead to lower spatial gradients of concentration for any instant of time, but also result in a higher difference of concentration between both cathode surfaces (cf. Fig. 4 (h, e, b)). This result is in agreement with what would be mathematically expected from Eq. (29c). Also, by observing Eq. (32) it is possible to conclude that such a pattern will result in a decrease of the overpotential sources related to the charge transport in the Cathode Zone, which is indeed observed in Fig. 4 (i, f, c).

It is important to notice, however, that even for Case 500.0 the sudden overpotential spike at the end of the test (which ultimately will stop the discharge routine) is still being driven by cathode reasons. This observation not only can be confirmed by the average level of concentration profiles (notice that a higher average concentration means that the battery finished the process more discharged) but also represents another evidence of the previously stated conclusion that cathode diffusion is one of the most determinant factors for battery devices to reach the ideal capacity expected from thermodynamics.

Furthermore, it is possible to perceive that the main overpotential source for Case 500.0 is still the mass transfer in the electrolyte zone (cf. Fig. 4(c)). This result highlights that even though the cathode diffusion was severely upgraded, the unaffected electrolyte will still present enough resistance to represent the major source of overpotential and ultimately prevent the discharge curve from reaching the Nernst Curve. Nonetheless, even though this overpotential source kept practically unchanged, it was still possible to reach a dimensionless average concentration near unity (cf. Fig. 4(a)), therefore illustrating the fact that it is possible to reach (very closely) the theoretical capacity of the battery just by increasing the diffusion of the Cathode Zone, despite still presenting some voltage drop (i.e. energy inefficiency) mainly due to the overpotential from the unaffected Electrolyte Zone.

Finally, to study the individual contributions of the ion diffusion and electron diffusion in such a model, a similar parametric study was performed, this time changing only
D
M
⊕
or
D
e
−
. The results are presented in Fig. 5.

From Fig. 5 it is possible to notice that the individual impact of Electron Diffusion (
D
e
−
) is significantly lower than the impact of Ion Diffusion (
D
M
⊕
) in the discharge curves — which is in agreement with the assumption that the ion transport along the Cathode Zone is the limiting step and therefore increasing/decreasing the Electron Diffusion (
D
e
−
) will not meaningfully impact the discharge curves. The Ion Diffusion (
D
M
⊕
), on the other hand, is enough to provide a similar influence to the one observed by modifying both of them (cf. Fig. 3).

The mathematical explanation for this is that multiplying the individual values of each parameter by a constant K is the same as decreasing the value of the denominator of the equivalent diffusion constant (cf. Eq. (30)) by a factor of

Therefore, such an explanation implies that for materials in which the electroneutrality hypothesis is valid, and the diffusion coefficient of one species is significantly higher than the other, the mass transport will be mostly dictated by the species with the lowest diffusion — in this case, the ion

Since the electroneutrality condition is also considered for the Electrolyte Zone, it is possible to study the variation of the equivalent diffusion constant in the electrolyte (
D

), or individually focus on the diffusion constant of the ions (

Li

) or the diffusion constant of the electrolyte vacancies (

) adopting a methodology akin to that for the Cathode Zone. Notice that, for the Electrolyte Zone, the counter-ion is not the electron particle but a negative vacancy left from the ion hopping movement [10,20].

Starting with the analysis of both (
D

+
) and (

) simultaneously, the standard values of such diffusion constants (cf. Table 3) were multiplied by a factor from 0.1 to 500.0 - which is the numerically alternative of varying the “equivalent” diffusion constant in the electrolyte (

) directly. The results are presented in Fig. 6. Similarly to what was stated for the Cathode Zone, for the sake of convenience, the term “electrolyte diffusion” will refer to the equivalent diffusion coefficient (
D
s
) unless stated otherwise.

Fig. 6 reveals that electrolyte diffusion significantly influences the electrical potential during discharge, yet, unlike cathode diffusion studies, its variation has a minimal effect on Extraction Efficiency. For instance, Case 0.2 showed an Extraction Efficiency of 64.69%, and Case 500.0 exhibited 64.97%, indicating a negligible difference despite extensive parameter variation, underscoring the electrolyte diffusion’s limited role in enhancing battery capacity. Conversely, Voltaic Efficiency experienced a notable increase from approximately 83.0% in Case 0.2 to about 97.8% in Case 500.0.

It is noticeable, however, that the efficacy of enhancing electrolyte diffusion on improving Voltaic Efficiency tapers off as the discharge curves reach a threshold, beyond which overpotential reduction through this variable is not feasible — as evidenced by the consistent outcomes between Cases 20–500. The remaining disparity between Case 500 and the Equilibrium Curve was tracked by source, and the results are displayed in Fig. 7.

Fig. 7 elucidates that the observed deviation from the Equilibrium Curve cannot be ascribed to a singular overpotential source. Instead, it results from a confluence of other potential losses, notably those associated with the cathode zone and the charge-transfer processes inherent in electrochemical reactions. This comprehensive analysis underscores the multifaceted nature of the contributing elements to the system’s overall behavior.

Subsequently, the individual roles of ion diffusion in the electrolyte zone (
D
M
+
) and the diffusion coefficient of vacancies (
D
n
−
) can be explored using a similar approach to that employed for the Cathode Zone, as represented in Fig. 8.

Considering the explanation in the Cathode Section (cf. Eq. (43)) it is natural to assume that a similar effect would be observed for the Electrolyte Zone. However, it is possible to observe from Fig. 8 that both parameters are relevant to the discharge profile.

This is mostly explained by the fact that, in this case, both constants are in the same order of magnitude (cf. Table 3), and therefore none of them act as a limiting step, resulting in both of them significantly contributing to the equivalent diffusion constant in the electrolyte (
D
s
). Such insight can also be confirmed by the analogous mathematical deduction similar to the one presented in Eq. (43). As expected, for both cases, increasing the values of such coefficients led to higher electrical potentials, closer to the Equilibrium Curve, and therefore higher Voltaic Efficiencies when compared to cases in which such coefficients were decreased — such KPI was increased from 90.38% in Case 0.2 (a) to 97.36% in Case 500.0 (a), and 93.05% in Case 0.2 (b) to 97.7% in Case 500.0 (b).

Another notable difference is that, in the case of Electrolyte Zone, the boundary conditions are based only on the Ion Diffusion (cf. Eq. (23b) and Eq. (23c)), which is in agreement with the fact that there is no electron transfer in any of its surfaces (differently, for example, to the surface Cathode/Current-Collector). This means that for an Ion Diffusion sufficiently high, the concentration immediately at the surface will present higher values, potentially impacting other overpotential sources. This can be observed by the Voltaic Efficiency at the limiting Case 500.0 (97.36% for Vacancy Diffusion, and 97.7% for Ion Diffusion) and in the overpotential profiles, through a slightly lower total overpotential resulting from, among other factors, a lower charge-transfer overpotential, as shown in Fig. 9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parametric study: Cathode and electrolyte diffusion
ce:section.ce:para:
This section presents the consequences of simultaneously altering the diffusion parameters for four distinct species: ions and electrons in the Cathode Zone (
D
M
⊕
and
D
e
−
), and ions and vacancies in the Electrolyte Zone (
D
M
+
and
D
n
−
). The approach aligns with methodologies outlined in preceding sections, where baseline values of such species specified in Table 3 were scaled by factors ranging from 0.2 to 500.0. All the other parameters were not modified. The C-Rate considered in such analysis was
− 1
. The results of these parameter adjustments are illustrated in Fig. 10.

As expected, Fig. 10 confirms that, by changing the diffusion coefficients of all four species, the discharge profile will reach values noticeably close to the Equilibrium Curve. For Case 500.0, it reached an Extraction Efficiency of 98.9% (vs. 64.6% for Case 1.0) and a Voltaic Efficiency of 99.0% (vs. 94.6% for Case 1.0), demonstrating that – according to the considered model, and considering the standard parameters presented in Table 3 – it is possible to achieve values close to the thermodynamic limits of solid-state batteries just by improving the materials’ diffusion constants i.e. without changing the concentration of ions both in the Cathode and the Electrolyte Zone neither kinetic constants.

It is also possible to observe that eventually, increasing such parameters converge to a threshold that cannot be surpassed purely by increasing diffusion parameters (cf. the proximity of Case 500.0 and Case 100.0). By observing the remaining overpotential sources for Case 500.0, it is possible to imply that such threshold is imposed mainly by charge-transfer resistances and, since the total overpotential is small enough, even the ohmic resistance of the lithium metal anode and the platinum current collectors start to be relevant — as presented in Fig. 11.

The next phase of our analysis focused on the kinetic constants for both the positive (
k
1
) and negative (
k
2
) reactions. This was accomplished by individually scaling their standard values (cf. Table 3) by a multiplier ranging from
5 ⋅ 1
0
− 7
to
1 ⋅ 1
0
5
and the C-Rate was consistently set to −1. All the other parameters listed in Table 3 remained unchanged, and the discharge curves are presented in Fig. 12.

As expected, by increasing the kinetic constant it is observed a decrease in the difference between the discharge curves and the equilibrium curve, which translates to an increase in Voltaic Efficiency — 76.8% in “Case
5 ⋅ 1
0
− 7
” vs 95.22% in “Case
1
0
5
” for the Positive Kinetic study (a) and from 77.15% in “Case
5 ⋅ 1
0
− 7
” vs 95.0% in “Case
1
0
5
” for the Negative Kinetic study (b).

Also, for all results presented in Fig. 12, the Extraction Efficiency is kept in ca. 64.61% — this finding, again, supports the notion that the battery’s effective capacity is predominantly influenced by cathode properties, such as diffusion and concentration, within the parameter range examined in this study.

It is important to notice that, in this work, the Extraction Efficiency is measured during the whole period in which the simulated device is discharged. In case the charge–discharge cycle is being limited e.g. at a 3.0 V lower boundary, this would imply that for extremely low values of kinetic constants, the overpotential would be so high that it would prematurely cease the test and, therefore, limit the Extraction Efficiency at 53.5% in (a) and 55.9% in (b).
Finally, it is possible to extract the Overpotential Profile, of Cases 0.1 and 100,000 of both studies, as represented in Fig. 13.

In Fig. 13, it is possible to conclude, as expected, that the major consequence of such an increase in Voltaic Efficiency is due to the respective charge-transfer overpotential for each study. Furthermore, the other overpotential sources remained approximately the same as expected from the conclusions of the last sections.

In the absence of side reactions, every reduction/oxidation event occurring at the cathode is mirrored by an oxidation/reduction reaction at the anode, ensuring that the influx of ions (
I
+
) into the Electrolyte Zone matches the efflux (
I
−
), thereby maintaining ion balance across the region (cf. Eq. (23b) and Eq. (23c)). Consequently, an increase in the amount of (free) mobile ions (or, as in this case, an increase in the concentration of a fixed volume) should enhance the current transport capability, potentially reducing the electrolyte’s mass transport overpotential.

In this section, we varied the concentration of vacancy sites in the Electrolyte Zone (
a
0
) by applying a multiplier to the baseline value from Table 3, ranging between 0.35 and 500 times. Given the constant parameters for the electrolyte dissociation reaction, changes in vacancy site concentration proportionally altered the initial ion concentration (see Eq. (23a)), effectively varying the electrolyte’s ionic concentration. The outcomes of this variation are depicted in Fig. 14, with all discharges conducted at a C-Rate of −1 unless otherwise noted.

As expected, Fig. 14 underscores the critical role of vacancy/ion concentration in optimizing electrolyte performance and efficiency within electrochemical cells. Also, it is possible to observe that increasing the concentration of ions/vacancies (
a
0
) decreases overpotentials up to a threshold point. Beyond this point, the ionic concentration is sufficiently high relative to the ionic current, making further increases ineffective in reducing overpotentials, as evidenced by the negligible differences between Case 20.0 and Case 500.0. The observed effect can be phenomenologically explained by noting that at high ion/vacancy concentrations relative to the ionic current required for electrochemical reactions, further increases in these concentrations lead to diminishing returns on the process efficiency. Consequently, this results in a reduced influence on the discharge curves.

Mathematically, this behavior is corroborated by examining the definition of the electrical field within the Electrolyte Zone (Eq. (25)), where a higher ion concentration consistently lowers the electrical field, thus diminishing mass transfer overpotential (Eq. (26)). Notably, while the Voltaic Efficiency increased significantly (from 87.3% in Case 0.35 to 98.6% in Case 500), these modifications did not markedly impact the Extraction Efficiency, which remained approximately at 65%. This indicates that similarly to electrolyte diffusion effects, adjustments to the electrolyte ion/vacancy concentration do not significantly enhance battery capacity under the studied conditions.

As discussed previously, the battery’s ideal capacity is proportional to the number of ions possible to be stored in the Cathode Zone (cf. Eq. (36)). Thus, increasing the maximum ion concentration in the Cathode (
a
M
⊕
max
) would not only impact such metric but also would proportionally impact the C-Rate calculation. With that in mind, there are two possible ways to study the impact of such parameter: (i) by also varying the C-Rate, in an inverse ratio, so the external electrical current remains constant or (ii) by keeping the C-Rate constant and therefore accepting that the external electrical current will be variable.

Starting with procedure (i), we adjusted the maximum ion concentration in the Cathode Zone (
a
M
⊕
max
) by modifying the standard value listed in Table 3 with a factor ranging from 0.2 to 500. To keep the external electrical current constant, the C-Rate for each Case was also multiplied by a factor from 1/0.2 to 1/500. The results are presented in Fig. 15.

We can see in Fig. 15 that the Energy Efficiency increased by a similar reason to the one explained for the Electrolyte Zone — for external currents (
I
bat
) high enough, the amount of free mobile species can be limited to the transport of such charges, and therefore increasing it can be beneficial up to some extent. Thus, even with the same diffusion parameters (and ion/vacancy concentration in the electrolyte zone) Fig. 16 shows that it is indeed possible to reach an Extraction Efficiency significantly close to ideality just by increasing the amount of mobile ions in the Cathode Zone.

However, in this case, this would also imply that the C-Rate would be decreased, reducing the usefulness of a device able to store such a high amount of energy (both due to higher theoretical capacity and Extraction Efficiency) without being able to charge it at an acceptable rate.

To avoid such a situation, it is possible to perform similar tests but without correcting the values of C-Rate, therefore increasing the external current to keep with the cathode’s new maximum capacity. Another parametric analysis was done in such context, and the results are displayed in Fig. 16.

It is possible to observe in Fig. 16 that for all cases studied, the battery discharging was limited to approximately the same value of x in M
x
L. Consequently, the Energy Extraction was kept between ca. 64.6%–64.7% for all cases, which corroborates with the idea that battery (real) capacity is mainly determined by cathode properties – such as diffusion and concentration – for the range of parameter values considered in this study.

Therefore, it is possible to increase the external battery current of a device without harming Energy Extraction just by increasing the maximum ion concentration in the Cathode Zone i.e. without improving the material diffusion.

However, despite higher concentrations of M
+
could theoretically improve some parameters in favor of lower overpotentials, it is possible to observe in Fig. 16 that the increase in
a
M
⊕
max
actually resulted in a decrease in the battery electrical potential during the discharge curves. As an example, Case 5.0 displayed a Voltaic Efficiency of 79.62% vs 94.64% in Case 1.0 (i.e. using the Standard Values of Table 3). Such results may seem unexpected if taken into consideration what was discussed in Fig. 15, or even the fundamental principle that cathode concentration may directly decrease other overpotential sources such as charge-transfer (cf. Eq. (5)).
To explain such phenomena, the concentration profile of ML (i.e. ions inside the cathode structure) and the overpotential profile of the battery device (specified by source) are displayed in Fig. 17.

From the concentration profiles displayed in Fig. 17 (left, middle), it is possible to conclude that despite the cathode concentration directly affecting the battery capacity (cf. Eq. (36)), if the range of x in
M
x
L
is kept constant and the same C-Rate is applied, the impact of such changes on the dimensionless concentration is negligible.

Such effect can also be mathematically justified by the boundary conditions of Eq. (29c), which states that the gradient of concentration in the spatial boundaries will be proportional to the electrical current. On another hand, the dimensionless concentration is always normalized against the maximum ion concentration in the Cathode Zone. Therefore, despite the electrical current being linearly increased in this study, the gradient of the dimensionless concentration is linearly decreased due to higher values of maximum ion concentration, ultimately resulting in the unchanged dimensionless concentration profile exhibited in Fig. 17.

This means that the increased overpotential values observed in Fig. 16 are not caused by phenomena related to the Cathode Zone — which is confirmed by the fact that the Overpotential Sources related to the positive electrode were approximately constant for all three cases. On another hand, it is possible to observe in Fig. 17 (c, f, i) a considerably higher value of overpotential related to the mass transfer in the Electrolyte Zone, which reaches a value of ca. 77% of all overpotential sources for Case 5.0.

Thus, it is possible to conclude that the increase in overpotential is not caused by a worse performance from the Cathode itself but by the fact that the higher maximum concentration in the Cathode Zone implied an increase in the external current (to keep C-Rate constant) and ultimately increased the voltage losses of the electrolyte material which, with unaltered properties, became the new bottleneck of the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parametric study: Cathode and electrolyte concentration
ce:section.ce:para: Since it was shown in Fig. 14 that it is possible to decrease the electrolyte resistance by increasing its ion/vacancy concentration, and it was shown in Figs. 15 and 16 that increasing the cathode concentration will increase battery capacity despite harming Voltaic Efficiency due to the electrolyte resistance, it is worthwhile to study the simultaneous variation of both parameters to answer if it is possible to obtain batteries with higher capacity and efficiency just by using materials with higher concentrations, without any modification to the diffusion parameters.

So, extending the approach of earlier sections, the maximum concentration in the Cathode Zone (
a
M
⊕
max
) and the initial concentration in the Electrolyte Zone (
a
0
) were simultaneously multiplied by values from 0.1 to 10.0, while maintaining all other parameters presented in Table 3 constant. The analysis was conducted at a standard C-Rate of −1, and the results are displayed in Fig. 18.

Previous sections have shown that increasing the cathode maximum concentration tends to increase the device capacity, but to keep the C-Rate constant, the electrolyte resistance will decrease voltaic efficiency. On another hand, it was also shown that increasing the electrolyte concentration tends to decrease the electrolyte resistance. Thus, by increasing both simultaneously it is natural to expect a battery with lower overpotentials, higher capacity, and higher voltaic efficiency — even for higher external currents (i.e. same C-Rate). Such a trend is indeed observed in Fig. 18 from Case 0.1 to 1. Also, as extensively explained in the previous section, neither the variation of electrolyte initial concentration nor the variation of cathode maximum concentration (for the same C-Rate) affected significantly the Extraction Efficiency. In this section, by varying both simultaneously, such an index was kept in ca. 64.6%, which is also an expected result.

However, an unexpected trend was observed from Case 1 to 10 — where the total overpotential increased even though the electrolyte concentration was also being raised. As a consequence, the Voltaic Efficiency dropped from 94.6% in Case 1.0 to 92.9% in Case 10.0, which presented the lowest value of such an index.
To explain such behavior, the Overpotential Profiles of three cases (Case 0.1, Case 1.0, and Case 10.0) are illustrated in Fig. 19.

From Fig. 19, it is possible to see that, indeed, increasing the electrolyte concentration resulted in the stabilization of the mass transfer overpotential in the electrolyte zone for all cases — which is in agreement with the previous discussion about such parameter. Also, for low concentrations (i.e. Case 0.1), the lower amount of compounds in the surface between the Cathode and Electrolyte Zones jeopardized the kinetic reactions and therefore increased the charge-transfer overpotential (cf. its definition in Eq. (3)).

Thus, by increasing both concentrations as in Case 1.0, the positive charge-transfer overpotential decreased and the voltaic losses from Electrolyte and Cathode Zones kept a similar pattern even though the external current was increased (to keep C-Rate constant).

This result showcases that, to some extent, it is indeed possible to simultaneously enhance battery capacity and increase the electrical current, while still keeping the overpotentials under control just by increasing concentration parameters i.e. without any chance in diffusion constants.

Finally, although Fig. 19(c) still shows the decreasing trend in positive charge-transfer overpotential and stable electrolyte mass transfer overpotential, the total overpotential is observed to increase. This happened because, to keep the C-Rate constant, the external electrical current is still being increased for every Case due to the higher theoretical capacities in the Cathode. As a consequence, other battery zones – that were previously considered irrelevant – progressively started to become the new bottleneck: in this case, the metal anode and the current collector. This result highlights the fact that a battery is an integrated, holistic device, in which even if the existing bottleneck is improved, another part of it will become the new bottleneck. On the other hand, a more optimistic point of view is that not only its performance will always be limited by its own materials, but that any battery device will always have room for improvement.

In this study, we demonstrated that Voltaic Efficiency can be significantly improved by enhancing electrolyte or kinetic properties alone, even though these factors are less effective at improving Extraction Efficiency. We also showed that nearly ideal Extraction Efficiency can be achieved by improving cathode diffusion. Additionally, we discussed how ideal Extraction Efficiency can be attained by increasing the maximum ion concentration in the cathode, even though it would be necessary to keep the absolute current constant (therefore decreasing C-Rate). We also examined how the decision to keep the C-Rate constant or variable may impact not only the performance measured by the KPIs but also the usefulness of such a device in practice. Furthermore, we explored how simultaneously varying pairs of parameters (e.g., initial electrolyte concentration and maximum ion concentration in the cathode) can reveal interesting scenarios that may not emerge when studying each parameter in isolation. The ideal discharge, represented by the Nernst Curve, was not achieved in any of the scenarios previously described.

In this section, one last scenario is demonstrated where all the parameters studied in this work were varied: the ion diffusion in the cathode (
D
M
⊕
), the electronic diffusion in the cathode (
D
e
−
), the ion diffusion in the electrolyte (
D
M
+
), the vacancy diffusion in the electrolyte (
D
n
−
), the positive kinetic constant (
k
1
), the negative kinetic constant (
k
2
), the initial concentration in the electrolyte (
a
0
), and the maximum ion concentration in the cathode (
a
M
⊕
max
).

To do so, and following the approach that aligns with methodologies in preceding sections, the baseline values of such parameters displayed in Table 3 were multiplied by factors ranging from 0.5 to 500. All the other parameters were kept constant, and the results are displayed in Fig. 20.

Fig. 20 shows that, as expected, when all diffusion, concentration, and kinetic parameters are enhanced simultaneously, the discharge curve approaches the thermodynamic limit represented by the Equilibrium Curve. Notably, the curves for Cases 20, 100, and 500 nearly overlap, suggesting that with simultaneous enhancement, the individual increase of each parameter does not need to be as large as in the individual studies. Finally, the concentration profile of ML (i.e. ions inside the cathode structure) and the overpotential profile of the battery device – specified by source – are displayed in Fig. 21.

In Fig. 21 (g, d, a) a flattening effect can be observed, similar to what is seen in Fig. 4, where only the diffusion in the Cathode Zone was enhanced (
D
M
⊕
and
D
e
−
). As a result, the overpotential profiles in Fig. 21 (i, f, c) show a reduction in the sources related to species transport within the Cathode Zone. Additionally, the variation in electrolyte mass transfer between cases is more pronounced, primarily due to the combined effects of

,

and

enhancements, in a similar fashion to what was demonstrated in Fig. 9 and Fig. 14, respectively.

Moreover, Fig. 21(c) indicates that in this hypothetical scenario, not only could an overpotential profile close to zero be achieved (i.e., Voltaic Efficiency approaching 100%), but the discharge process would also continue until a higher Extracted Charge value is reached (cf. x-axis), implying that Extraction Efficiency would also approach 100%. Finally, the total overpotential (marked by the black line) fades off before reaching values as low as in the other cases. This is because the discharge process is terminated based on absolute potential values. Since this discharge coincides with the Equilibrium Curve, even more neutral overpotential values are sufficient to signal the end of the discharge process, as confirmed by the near-unity average concentration profile in Fig. 21(b).

In this study, we proposed a model to represent any battery composed of a metallic anode, solid electrolyte, and intercalation electrode. This model not only predicts the dynamic electrical behavior of such batteries but also allows for the study and understanding of variations in concentration and electrical fields within the components responsible for this behavior.

We applied the model to estimate the material properties of a Li/LiPON/
LiCoO
2
battery dataset, which includes data from four discharge curves at different C-Rates. The simulated results showed a maximum deviation of approximately 5% compared to the experimental discharge data. Additionally, the estimated parameters were consistent with both experimental and simulated results found in the literature.

An in-depth parametric analysis was conducted to investigate the influence of diffusion, concentration, and electrochemical kinetics on the concentration profiles within the battery components, discharge curves, and key performance indicators (KPIs) such as Voltaic Efficiency and Extraction Efficiency.

Our investigation elucidates that among the various parameters evaluated, the cathode diffusion emerges as a paramount factor for augmenting the Extraction Efficiency of the device. It is demonstrated that, in the conditions studied, one can attain nearly the ideal capacity predicted by the Nernst Curve by solely enhancing this parameter, underscoring the importance of such property, especially for material developers aiming for higher capacities such as electric vehicles.

Under the conditions considered in this work, it is also worth highlighting the significance of electrolyte diffusion in elevating voltaic efficiency. This is primarily attributed to its role in mitigating mass transfer overpotential despite its impact on Extraction Efficiency appearing negligible.

This work also discussed that both diffusion properties, in the lens of the electroneutrality hypothesis, result from the interplay between electron/vacancy transport and ion diffusion and are predominantly influenced by the slower diffusing species.

Since the Extraction Efficiency is calculated from the beginning of the discharge until the moment in which the overpotential reaches infinity, the impact of electrolyte diffusion on Extraction Efficiency appears negligible. However, even though the amount of ions possible to insert in the cathode (without demanding such overpotentials) remains the same, some charge–discharge routines often limit such cycles using upper and lower boundaries of electrical potential. In this sense, higher overpotentials, such as the ones caused by low electrolyte diffusion or extremely low kinetic constants may jeopardize battery capacity.

The study also demonstrates that enhancing the concentration of cathode and electrolyte ions can effectively reduce mass transfer overpotential in both regions, significantly boosting Voltaic Efficiency, especially for the electrolyte. Increasing cathode ion concentration fundamentally enhances its capacity and, by maintaining a constant absolute current value, not only improves Extraction Efficiency to the point of eventually reaching values extremely close to the cathode’s ideal capacity (ca. 98.9%) but also augments Voltaic Efficiency (up to ca. 96.4%, for a maximum concentration of 500x higher the standard value). However, this approach also implies a reduction in the C-Rate, raising concerns regarding the usefulness of a device demanding such a time-consuming charge/discharge to take advantage of its full capacity. Conversely, maintaining a constant C-Rate, through an increase in absolute current, implicates other battery components such as the electrolyte and, in extreme cases, even the current collectors as critical factors, illustrating the battery as a holistic entity that will inherently exhibit bottlenecks but, at the same time, implying the perpetual potential for enhancement across its components
